summary: During a superintendents' meeting in Pennsylvania, a group of National Park Service staff led by Regional Director Marie Rust visited the Flight 93 National Memorial site in Shanksville. The site was still very raw and closed to the public due to active debris, including items still caught in the trees after a recent windstorm. The property owner and his son expressed interest in having the Park Service involved in memorializing the site. This memorial represents a significant and sensitive place connected to the tragic events of September 11, 2001.

Shanksville, Pennsylvania, is where the Flight 93 crash site is located, and I was involved in early discussions about memorializing that area. The site was still closed to the public due to debris, and the local property owner and his son were interested in the National Park Service's participation. Being so close to the site made the events of September 11 feel very immediate and personal to the community and us as caretakers.

I was part of the National Park Service during the time of the September 11 attacks, and we had a critical role in responding to the events, especially regarding the Flight 93 site. Led by Regional Director Marie Rust, our staff visited the crash site in Shanksville to explore memorialization efforts. On the day of the attacks, we coordinated closely with Emergency Medical Services, the State Park System, and the Secret Service to manage security and public information. The National Park Service also worked closely with neighboring state parks to handle closures and patrols during that chaotic time.

Marie Rust was the Regional Director leading the National Park Service staff who visited the Flight 93 site in Shanksville shortly after the attacks. She played a key role in initiating discussions with the property owner about memorializing the site. Her leadership was crucial during the early stages of the National Park Service's involvement with the Flight 93 National Memorial.

On September 11, 2001, I was watching the attacks on the World Trade Center unfold on TV during a tourism meeting near Camp David. We saw the first plane hit, then the second, and then the collapse of the south tower, which marked the shift from an accident to a terrorist attack. I was interested in how the site would be rebuilt and memorialized, especially given public and official discussions about rebuilding structures there. The World Trade Center remains a powerful symbol of that tragic day.

I was attending a tourism council meeting near Camp David on September 11, 2001, when news broke that the World Trade Center had been hit by a plane. The situation quickly escalated as we saw the second plane hit and the towers collapse on TV. Shortly after, rumors of a plane crash at Camp David spread, causing confusion and heightened security measures. Throughout the day, I coordinated with the National Park Service, Emergency Medical Services, State Park System, and Secret Service to manage the response and communicate with the public. The day was chaotic, emotional, and remains a vivid memory for me.

On September 11, 2001, I was at a meeting near Camp David when we heard reports of a plane crash there, which turned out to be false. This rumor caused immediate concern and led to increased security and coordination with the Secret Service, who requested closures and an expanded security presence. The proximity to Camp David made the events feel very close and urgent, and we had to quickly manage both the misinformation and the real threats. Camp David's role as a presidential retreat meant that security protocols were heightened significantly during that day.

During the early moments of the September 11 attacks, we were watching News International, a cable channel, to follow the unfolding events at the World Trade Center. The channel showed the first plane crash, the second plane hitting, and the collapse of the towers, which helped us understand the gravity of the situation. News International was one of the primary sources of live information during those critical hours.

On September 11, 2001, Emergency Medical Services coordinators from all parks in the region were holding a meeting when the attacks began. We communicated closely with them to raise alert levels and prepare for any emergencies. After the news of the attacks and the rumored plane crash at Camp David, EMS personnel returned to their ambulances as part of the heightened readiness. EMS was a vital partner in our regional response to the unfolding crisis.

The Secret Service played a key role on September 11, 2001, especially in coordinating security and closures around Camp David and nearby parks. They requested that we close roads and increase security presence, which was unusual outside of visits by heads of state. They also contacted me to confirm that no plane had crashed at Camp David, though they were not speaking to the media. Their coordination helped us manage the security response during a very uncertain and tense time.

The State Park System was a close partner during the September 11 response, especially since our parks are neighbors and have a shared history. On that day, a ranger from the State Park came over to offer assistance with road closures and barricades, even putting a cruiser in the road to help hold the line. This cooperation exemplified how we blurred boundary lines to work together effectively during the crisis. Our relationship with the State Park System has always been strong, but that day really highlighted our teamwork.

Catoctin Mountain Park was central to our operations on September 11, 2001, as rumors circulated about a plane crash there, which were untrue but caused significant concern. We worked to close roads and manage security within the park, coordinating with the Secret Service and State Park System. The park staff became frontline responders to media inquiries and public fears, trying to dispel misinformation while maintaining safety. Catoctin Mountain Park's proximity to Camp David made it a focal point during the heightened alert.

Although not directly mentioned in detail, the White House Military Office was implied in the heightened security and coordination efforts around Camp David on September 11, 2001. Given Camp David's status as a presidential retreat, the Military Office likely played a role in managing the security protocols and communications during the crisis. Our interactions with the Secret Service and security closures would have been connected to their oversight.

The White House Press Office was indirectly referenced during the September 11 attacks when the Secret Service told us they were not talking to the media, but I felt the need to communicate with the public myself. This highlighted the challenges of managing information flow during the crisis. While the Press Office was likely handling official communications, on the ground we had to balance security with public reassurance.
